gm.name: Jerry Jones
gm.title: Owner / President / General Manager
gm.previous_role: Purchased franchise — no prior NFL front office role
gm.notes: Jerry Jones holds the GM title but Will McClay is the de facto draft decision-maker since 2014. The profile reflects the McClay-era philosophy (2014–present) as the operative behavioral model.

career_path.role: Player/Coach — Defensive Coordinator/Director of Player Personnel (Anaheim Piranhas, Milwaukee Mustangs, Florida Bobcats, Grand Rapids Rampage)
career_path.mentor: Self-developed; arena football roots in talent evaluation

career_path.role: Director of Player Personnel / Consultant / Scout

career_path.mentor: Jerry Jones organization; dual role NFL scouting and AFL coaching

career_path.role: Head Coach (also continuing Cowboys pro scout duties)
career_path.mentor: Jerry Jones; 55-28-1 record as AFL HC
Jerry Jones / Stephen Jones; analytics-adjacent role evaluating advanced scouting methods
Promoted after 2013 draft disconnect between scouts and coaches over Sharrif Floyd

career_path.role: Senior Director of Pro/College Scouting (overseeing draft board from 2014)
career_path.mentor: Jerry Jones / Stephen Jones — McClay now controls draft board
Jerry Jones / Stephen Jones — highest-ranking non-Jones member of front office

scouting_tree.primary_mentor: Self-made evaluator. McClay's background is unique — he came through arena football coaching and pro scouting rather than a traditional NFL scouting tree. His coaching background gives him a scheme-awareness dimension most pure scouts lack.
scouting_tree.philosophical_lineage: McClay is not descended from a traditional NFL scouting tree. His arena football and coaching roots mean he evaluates players with both a scout's eye for traits and a coach's eye for football IQ and scheme fit. The Bill Parcells era (2003-2006) in Dallas influenced the organization's respect for positional measurable thresholds. McClay has internalized this — he explicitly references 'criteria or standards that have been tried and true' for positional measurables.
scouting_tree.breakpoints: McClay's promotion after the 2013 draft (where coaches overruled scouts on Sharrif Floyd) was the foundational breakpoint. He resolved the scout-coach disconnect by ensuring scheme fit and talent are weighted together on the board. The Mazi Smith pick (2023) — where traits trumped film — appears to have reinforced McClay's growing caution about pure athletic projection without production.
scouting_tree.lineage_weight_notes: McClay has 12 drafts of his own data (2014-2025). Scouting tree lineage is nearly irrelevant; his own track record IS the profile. The lineage weight reflects only the residual Parcells-era measurable thresholds influence.

front_office.primary_decision_maker: Will McClay (de facto); Jerry Jones (final authority/title holder)

key_influencers.name: Stephen Jones
key_influencers.title: Chief Operating Officer / Executive Vice President / Co-Owner
key_influencers.known_biases: Conservative financial philosophy. Talked Jerry out of trading up for Paxton Lynch in 2016. Prioritizes salary cap discipline, which can lead to letting veterans walk and creating draft needs. Has pushed for drafting Zack Martin over Johnny Manziel in 2014. Values offensive line and fiscal restraint.

key_influencers.name: Brian Schottenheimer
key_influencers.title: Head Coach (since January 2025)
key_influencers.known_biases: Offensive-minded. Values run-game commitment and offensive line play. Hired Christian Parker as DC, indicating desire for multiple/3-4 defensive scheme. First year coaching in 2025, still establishing his draft-room influence. Jerry Jones praised the Schottenheimer-McClay dynamic in 2025 draft room.

key_influencers.name: Christian Parker
key_influencers.title: Defensive Coordinator (hired January 2026)
key_influencers.known_biases: Vic Fangio coaching tree (Eagles DBs coach/pass game coordinator). Wants 3-4 base with multiple fronts. Explicitly stated 'you build it around the players' — scheme flexibility over rigid positional requirements. Values versatile EDGE players who can stand up or put hand in dirt. First year as DC anywhere.

front_office.power_dynamics: Collaborative but Jerry Jones retains final say. McClay builds the draft board and effectively runs the personnel department. Stephen Jones manages cap and contracts, influencing which positions become draft needs. Jerry Jones has said 'I do not make the decision about that player. That's been made cumulatively.' However, Jerry can and does override — the Mazi Smith pick was reportedly influenced by Jerry's desire for a run-stuffing DT. The coaching staff has input but McClay's board drives selections.
front_office.owner_interference: high — Jerry Jones is the GM in title and very publicly involved. He makes draft-related comments to media that can move markets. He has veto power and uses it selectively. His son Stephen provides a moderating influence. Despite the title, the actual pick-by-pick process is driven by McClay, with Jerry's influence most felt on high-profile Round 1 selections and trade decisions.

draft_philosophy.stated_philosophy: McClay: 'We set our board regardless of free agency... We're not going to change based on needs. Let's set our board based off of our historical values and the way we value positions and players.' Also: 'It's a combination of the two [BPA and need]. It's how you navigate that and how you truly value the player. That way you don't push guys up the board because of a need.'
draft_philosophy.actual_philosophy: Leans BPA in round 1 but with significant need influence. CeeDee Lamb at 17 (2020) with Cooper and Gallup already on roster was the purest BPA pick. But Mazi Smith at 26 (2023) was a clear need pick for run defense. Tyler Booker at 12 (2025) addressed guard need despite WR Matthew Golden being available. Tyler Guyton at 29 (2024) addressed OT need. The pattern shows BPA within the context of premium positions, but when a clear roster hole exists at a premium position, need wins.
draft_philosophy.philosophy_mismatch_notes: McClay says the board is set independent of need, but the actual picks show need significantly influences Round 1 selections. Of the last 6 first-round picks (2020-2025): Lamb was BPA, Parsons addressed LB need AND was BPA, Tyler Smith addressed OT need, Mazi Smith addressed DT need, Guyton addressed OT need, Booker addressed IOL need. At least 4 of 6 directly addressed top-3 team needs.

trade_triggers: Trading back in Round 1 when a target is available later (2021: traded from 10 to 12; 2024: traded from 24 to 29)
Day 3 trades up for specific targets (2025: traded up for LB Shemar James)
Acquiring veterans via mid-round pick trades during season (Jonathan Mingo, Kenneth Murray, Quinnen Williams)
Jerry Jones willingness to trade first-round picks for proven veterans (Amari Cooper trade used a 1st)
trade_behavior.draft_capital_philosophy: Historically conservative — prefer to trade back and accumulate picks rather than trade up. Stephen Jones explicitly said he 'couldn't see Dallas trading up from No. 12 but could see a situation where we move down.' However, Jerry Jones signaled willingness to trade 2026 first-rounders for proven talent (Maxx Crosby rumors). The Cowboys have made 72+ draft-day trades under Jones ownership. They favor accumulating Day 2 and Day 3 picks through trade-backs.

position_avoidance: QB in early rounds — Dak Prescott was a 4th-round pick (2016). No other QB drafted in rounds 1-3 in McClay era.
Safety has never been drafted in rounds 1-2 under McClay. They find safeties in free agency or later rounds.
TE has been drafted once in the top 60 picks (Schoonmaker, 2nd round 2023). Not a premium draft position.
position_premium: OL is the signature position group. In 12 drafts of McClay era: Zack Martin (R1), Tyler Smith (R1), Tyler Guyton (R1), Tyler Booker (R1), Cooper Beebe (R3), plus numerous Day 3 OL picks. At least one OL drafted in 12 of 15 years.
EDGE/DL consistently addressed in rounds 1-3: DeMarcus Lawrence (R2 trade-up), Taco Charlton (R1), Sam Williams (R2), Marshawn Kneeland (R2), Donovan Ezeiruaku (R2), Mazi Smith (R1).
LB has been targeted in rounds 1-3 repeatedly: Jaylon Smith (R2), Leighton Vander Esch (R1), Micah Parsons (R1), DeMarvion Overshown (R3), Marist Liufau (R3).

EDGE.archetype_summary: Versatile, long-armed, high-motor edge rusher with scheme flexibility. Dallas drafts EDGE players who can operate as 4-3 DEs or stand-up OLBs. They prioritize length (34"+ arms), athleticism, and power-to-speed conversion. Production is valued but they will bet on raw athleticism from smaller schools if the physical tools are exceptional. The Kneeland and Ezeiruaku picks both fit this mold — long, explosive, power-based rushers from non-Power 5 programs.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 33 inches — all EDGE picks in rounds 1-3 have had 33"+ arms. Ezeiruaku: 34"; Kneeland: 34.5"; Parsons: 34"; Charlton: 33.5"
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Prioritizes burst metrics (vertical jump 34"+, broad jump 10'+). 3-cone and shuttle important for agility. Kneeland's 7.02 3-cone and 4.18 shuttle at 267 lbs were elite. Speed Score (weight-adjusted 40 time) matters.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.80 or faster at weight. Parsons ran 4.39 at 246; Kneeland ran 4.75 at 267; Ezeiruaku estimated mid-4.6s at 248. Explosiveness preferred over pure timed speed.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Speed-to-power conversion and versatility. Kneeland was described as having 'the most explosive speed-to-power conversion' in his class. Ezeiruaku wins with jab-step footwork and strong length. Parsons was a hybrid LB/EDGE prototype.
most_valued_traits: Length usage and heavy hands at point of attack
Scheme versatility — can rush from 2-point or 3-point stance
Run defense ability — must set the edge
least_important_traits: Polished pass-rush repertoire — they draft raw rushers and develop them (Kneeland had limited moves, Ezeiruaku was a projection)
deal_breakers: Stiff hips and inability to redirect — see Taco Charlton bust
Lack of length — no sub-33" arms in early rounds
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite athleticism at the position can cause a reach. Kneeland at pick 56 was above most consensus boards (viewed as R3 player by some). McClay has acknowledged 'traits get you in trouble' but continues to bet on physical profiles.

college_production.production_floor: No hard sack minimum — Kneeland had only 12.5 career sacks at Western Michigan (MAC). Ezeiruaku had 16.5 sacks in final season (ACC). They will take lower production from smaller schools if traits are exceptional.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers ascending production. Ezeiruaku's breakout senior season (16.5 sacks) was the ideal trajectory. Kneeland improved each year at Western Michigan.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — they adjust heavily for competition level. Two of their last three EDGE picks (Kneeland from Western Michigan, Ezeiruaku from Boston College) came from non-elite programs but showed traits against better competition at Senior Bowl/Combine.

examples: 2025 R2.44 — Donovan Ezeiruaku: 6-2, 248, 34" arms. 16.5 sacks at Boston College. Wins with footwork (jab step) and length. Power-first projection.
2024 R2.56 — Marshawn Kneeland: 6-3, 267, 34.5" arms. 4.75/40, 7.02 3-cone. Speed-to-power converter with elite combine. MAC product.
2022 R2.56 — Sam Williams: 6-4, 261. 12.5 sacks at Ole Miss. Character risk (fell from R1). Long, explosive rusher.
2021 R1.12 — Micah Parsons: 6-3, 246, 34" arms. 4.39/40. Hybrid LB/EDGE. Generational athleticism. Penn State.
2017 R1.28 — Taco Charlton: 6-6, 277. Michigan. Long, powerful profile but lacked agility — the bust that teaches what NOT to draft.
2014 R2.34 — DeMarcus Lawrence: 6-3, 251. Boise State. Traded up to get him. Power rusher who became All-Pro.

OT.archetype_summary: Long, athletic, high-upside offensive tackle with position flexibility. Dallas drafts OTs who are projections — raw but extraordinarily athletic, with the length and movement skills to develop into franchise tackles. They accept technical rawness in exchange for elite physical tools. Tyler Guyton (6-8, 322) and Tyler Smith (6-5, 324) are the archetype: massive frames, elite athleticism for their size, limited college starts but enormous ceilings.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 34 inches — Guyton and Smith both had 34"+ arms. This is effectively a hard cutoff for round 1 OTs.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Vertical jump (explosiveness), 3-cone (agility for size). Guyton had 97th-percentile vertical jump and 84th-percentile 3-cone. Athletic upside is the priority.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Not a primary factor, but must show ability to mirror speed rushers on film. Sub-5.10 40 preferred but not required.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Athletic pass protectors who can develop technique. Dallas accepts technical rawness if the athletic ceiling is elite.
most_valued_traits: Athletic movement skills for the size — lateral agility, mirror ability
Upside/ceiling over floor — willing to develop raw players
least_important_traits: Immediate NFL-readiness — they are willing to develop OTs slowly (Guyton was a project with 15 career college starts)
deal_breakers: Short arms (sub-34" for tackles)
Stiff mover who cannot mirror athletic pass rushers
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite athleticism for the size. Guyton was considered a reach at 29 by some (projected late R1/early R2) but his combine athleticism drove the pick.

college_production.production_floor: Low — Guyton had only 15 career college starts. Tyler Smith had limited experience at Tulsa. Dallas is willing to bet on traits over college starts at OT.
college_production.production_trajectory: No strong preference — they will take one-year starters if the athletic profile is elite.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Moderate — they have taken OTs from both Power 5 (Oklahoma) and Group of 5 (Tulsa, Louisiana-Lafayette) programs.

examples: 2024 R1.29 — Tyler Guyton: 6-8, 322, Oklahoma. 97th-percentile vertical, 84th-percentile 3-cone. Raw with only 15 college starts. Elite athletic projection.
2022 R1.24 — Tyler Smith: 6-5, 324, Tulsa. Moved from LT to LG seamlessly as rookie. Athletic, powerful, versatile. Became 2x Pro Bowler.
2025 R6.204 — Ajani Cornelius: Oregon. Swing tackle with 50 career starts. Position flexibility valued.
2024 R7.233 — Nathan Thomas: 6-5, 334, Louisiana-Lafayette. Size and versatility (tackle/guard flex).

IOL.archetype_summary: Physical, versatile interior lineman who can play multiple positions. Dallas values guards and centers who can cross-train — play guard and center interchangeably. They want size, power, and enough athleticism to execute zone-blocking concepts with run-game physicality. Tyler Booker (2025) and Cooper Beebe (2024) represent the archetype: big, physical, position-flexible interior linemen from strong programs.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No strict minimum for interior — Beebe had shorter arms but elite technique. Booker at 6-5, 325 provides ideal size.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Bench press (functional strength), short shuttle (short-area quickness for pulling and combo blocks). Less emphasis on 40 time than OT.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Not a primary factor. Must show ability to pull and reach second level.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Physical run blockers who can also anchor in pass protection. The OL tradition in Dallas demands both power and technique.
most_valued_traits: Consistent technique — Booker allowed only 1 sack in 970 pass-blocking snaps
least_important_traits: Elite athleticism — they prioritize technique and consistency over combine numbers for IOL
deal_breakers: Poor anchor in pass protection
Lack of competitive toughness or football character
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Exceptional consistency and technique. Booker at 12 overall was taken over skill position needs because of his floor as a Day 1 starter.

college_production.production_floor: Multi-year starter preferred. Booker had 970+ pass-blocking snaps. Beebe was a multi-year starter at Kansas State.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers experienced multi-year starters who are NFL-ready.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Values players from strong offensive line programs (Alabama, Kansas State, Notre Dame).

examples: 2025 R1.12 — Tyler Booker: 6-5, 325, Alabama. 1 sack allowed in 970 pass-blocking snaps. Immediate starter. 0.8% blown-block rate.
2024 R3.73 — Cooper Beebe: Kansas State. Guard/center versatility. Moved to center in 2025. Physical, high-IQ lineman.
2014 R1.16 — Zack Martin: Notre Dame. Played OT in college, moved to guard. 9x Pro Bowler. The gold standard of this archetype.
2013 R1.31 — Travis Frederick: Wisconsin. Center. Technically excellent. 5x Pro Bowler.

DT.archetype_summary: Big, powerful interior defender who can stuff the run and generate interior pressure. Dallas values size and strength at DT — they want players who can hold the point of attack and two-gap. The Mazi Smith pick (2023) was the extreme version: a physical freak (topped the Athletic's Freaks List) with elite combine numbers but limited production. This was widely viewed as a miss, and McClay has since acknowledged 'traits get you in trouble.' The correction suggests future DT picks will require more production to match the traits.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 33 inches — McClay has stated 'it comes down to size and length more than anything' for defensive linemen.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Bench press (strength). Mazi Smith had 34 reps at 323 lbs. Short-area quickness matters but they prioritize anchor strength.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Not a primary factor. Must show quick first step on film but timed speed is secondary to power.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Run defense first, interior pressure second. McClay has been explicit that DT picks are about improving run defense.
most_valued_traits: Size and mass to hold point of attack
Ability to collapse the pocket from interior
Scheme versatility — can play nose or 3-technique
least_important_traits: Sack production — they don't need DTs to be primary pass rushers. Interior pressure is a bonus, not a requirement.
deal_breakers: Getting pushed off the ball consistently in the run game
Lack of motor or competitive effort
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite athletic testing at size. Mazi Smith's freakish combine numbers caused a Round 1 reach that didn't pay off. This lesson may temper future trait-over-production bets at DT.

college_production.production_floor: Low — Mazi Smith had minimal sack production at Michigan but was a dominant run defender. The Cowboys appear willing to draft DTs with low sack numbers if the run defense grade is elite.
college_production.production_trajectory: No strong preference. They are willing to take run-stuffers with limited pass-rush production.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Context matters — Smith played on a loaded Michigan defense where he was asked to occupy blocks.

examples: 2023 R1.26 — Mazi Smith: 6-3, 323, Michigan. 34 bench reps. Elite athletic freak but minimal production. Bust-level pick.
2025 R7.217 — Jay Toia: 325 lbs, UCLA. Run-stuffing nose tackle. Size over production.
2025 R7.247 — Tommy Akingbesote: Maryland. 33.5" arms. Raw but traits-based developmental pick.
2024 R7.244 — Justin Rogers: 6-3, 346, Auburn. Massive run-plugger.

DT.confidence_note: Only one high draft pick (Mazi Smith) and it was a clear miss. Day 3 DT picks suggest size-first philosophy. The 2026 scheme change to 3-4 may alter DT needs significantly.

CB.archetype_summary: Long, physical press-man cornerback with size to match NFC East receivers. Dallas consistently targets CBs with length (6-0+, 31"+ arms), physicality, and press-man technique. They have explicitly stated they 'want to have press-man corners.' The DaRon Bland (5th round steal) and Shavon Revel Jr. (3rd round) picks embody this: length, physicality, and the ability to jam at the line of scrimmage. Ball production is secondary to coverage ability.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 31 inches — Revel has 32 5/8" arms. DaRon Bland and Trevon Diggs both had 31"+ arms. Length is non-negotiable for outside CBs.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed is paramount — 4.50 40-yard dash appears to be the ceiling for early-round CBs. Diggs ran 4.42. Length/speed combo drives the evaluation.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.50 or faster strongly preferred. Exception may be made for elite length and physicality.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Press-man coverage ability. ESPN explicitly noted 'the Cowboys want to have press-man corners' when analyzing the Revel pick.
most_valued_traits: Length and physicality at the catch point
Frame to match up against big NFC East receivers (Philly, Washington)
least_important_traits: Zone instincts in isolation — press-man is the foundation of their CB evaluation
deal_breakers: Lack of length for outside CB role
Poor tackling — CBs must contribute in run defense
Slow feet that prevent mirroring in press coverage
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite length + speed combination. Revel at 6-3, 193 with 32 5/8" arms was taken in Round 3 despite coming off a torn ACL because the physical profile was too rare to pass on.

college_production.production_floor: No hard statistical floor. DaRon Bland had modest college stats but elite ball skills. Revel had 3 INTs in 2 years. They weight film over interception totals.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers multi-year starters but will take underclassmen with elite traits.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — they adjust for competition level and scheme. Caelen Carson from Wake Forest was a physical, run-defense-first CB from a physical program.

examples: 2025 R3.76 — Shavon Revel Jr.: 6-3, 193, 32 5/8" arms, East Carolina. Press-man specialist. Taken despite torn ACL. 3 INTs in 2 years as starter.
2024 R5.174 — Caelen Carson: Wake Forest. Physical prospect. 83.4 PFF run-defense grade. Ball skills.
2022 R5.167 — DaRon Bland: 5-11, 197, Fresno State. Led team with 5 INTs as rookie. All-Pro in 2023. 5th-round steal.
2020 R2.51 — Trevon Diggs: 6-1, 205, Alabama. Physical, ball-hawking CB. 2x Pro Bowler.
2015 R1.27 — Byron Jones: 6-1, 199, UConn. World-record broad jump. Elite athlete drafted as safety, moved to CB.

S.archetype_summary: Not a premium draft position for Dallas. The Cowboys have not used a top-100 pick on a pure safety in the McClay era. They find safeties in free agency (Jayron Kearse, Malik Hooker, Donovan Wilson) or convert other positions. When they do draft safeties, they want rangy, coverage-first players who can play deep centerfield or cover tight ends.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Range and speed are paramount. 40-yard dash matters for single-high ability. Closing speed on film is the primary athletic indicator.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.50 or faster preferred for free safety types.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Coverage versatility — man-to-man against TEs and zone coverage in deep halves.
most_valued_traits: Versatility to play multiple safety spots
Experience — prefer veterans at this position
least_important_traits: Blitz ability — not a priority for safety evaluation
deal_breakers: Poor angles in pursuit
Inability to cover tight ends in man coverage
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: N/A — Dallas does not reach for safeties in the draft. They fill this position through free agency.

college_production.production_floor: No established threshold — insufficient draft data at this position.

examples: 2021 R3.84 — Israel Mukuamu: 6-4, 211, South Carolina. Safety/CB hybrid. Rangy coverage player. Did not develop.
2018 R6.208 — Cedrick Wilson: Not a safety — illustrates how rarely Dallas drafts the position. Free agency is the primary pipeline.

S.confidence_note: Dallas has barely drafted safeties under McClay. Profile largely inferred from free agent targets (Kearse, Hooker, Wilson — all coverage-capable, versatile safeties). The 2026 scheme change to 3-4 under Parker may increase safety draft priority.

LB.archetype_summary: Rangy, athletic, coverage-capable linebacker — ideally a former safety or defensive back convert with sideline-to-sideline speed. Dallas heavily invests in linebackers who can cover in space. Micah Parsons (hybrid LB/EDGE), Leighton Vander Esch (rangy tackler), DeMarvion Overshown (converted safety), and Marist Liufau (hard-nosed run-and-coverage LB) all share athleticism and coverage ability as core traits. The 3-4 scheme change in 2026 will likely increase the importance of inside linebacker.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No strict minimum — varies by LB type
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: 40-yard dash and 3-cone are critical. Parsons (4.39), Overshown (4.59 at 229) — speed is paramount. Short shuttle for change of direction in coverage.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.65 or faster preferred. Must be able to run with tight ends and running backs in coverage.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Coverage ability and sideline-to-sideline range. Every LB drafted in rounds 1-3 under McClay has been an athlete-first selection.
most_valued_traits: Coverage ability against TEs and RBs
Versatility — can play multiple LB spots or flex to EDGE
least_important_traits: Pure run-stuffing ability in isolation — they want LBs who can do both but coverage is the differentiator
deal_breakers: Stiff hips that prevent coverage ability
Slow processor who cannot diagnose plays quickly enough
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite range and coverage instincts. Vander Esch at 19 (2018) was considered a slight reach by some but his sideline-to-sideline range drove the pick.

college_production.production_floor: High tackle volume preferred. Vander Esch had 141 tackles at Boise State. Overshown was a converted safety with growing tackle numbers.
college_production.production_trajectory: Ascending production preferred — Overshown transitioned from DB to LB and improved each year.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Values players who produced in multiple facets — tackles, TFLs, coverage stats.

examples: 2025 R5.152 — Shemar James: Florida. 20 years old. Athletic, blitzing ability. Traded up for him. Coverage skills developing.
2024 R3.87 — Marist Liufau: Notre Dame. 44 tackles, 6 TFL, 3 sacks final season. Hard-nosed with run-and-coverage ability.
2023 R3.90 — DeMarvion Overshown: 6-3, 229, Texas. Converted safety. Rangy coverage LB. Tore ACL before rookie year.
2021 R1.12 — Micah Parsons: 6-3, 246, Penn State. 4.39/40. Hybrid LB/EDGE. Generational.
2018 R1.19 — Leighton Vander Esch: 6-4, 256, Boise State. 141 tackles. Sideline-to-sideline range. Pro Bowl as rookie.
2016 R2.34 — Jaylon Smith: 6-2, 240, Notre Dame. Injury risk — torn ACL. Elite coverage LB when healthy.

WR.archetype_summary: Best player available when elite talent falls, or Day 3 athletic fliers. Dallas does NOT draft WRs for need — they take them when elite value falls (CeeDee Lamb at 17 with Cooper and Gallup already on roster) or on Day 3 as traits-based developmental picks. They have not used a top-50 pick on a WR since Lamb in 2020. The 2025 draft had zero WRs taken despite a known need. When they do draft WRs, they want big, fast, physical playmakers.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No strict minimum — Lamb is 6-2, but they also drafted Jalen Tolbert (6-1) and targeted smaller slot types
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed and explosiveness. Ryan Flournoy (2024 R6) stood out at combine with athletic testing. McClay values 'explosive playmakers.'
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.50 or faster preferred for outside WRs. Deep speed valued.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Playmaking ability after the catch and contested-catch ability. Lamb wins with route running and YAC. The Flournoy archetype (big/fast/physical) suggests they want Dez Bryant-style contested-catch ability on Day 3.
most_valued_traits: Size/speed combination for outside WR role
Scheme versatility — can play multiple WR spots
least_important_traits: Return ability — they find that separately (KaVontae Turpin)
Blocking — nice to have but not a draft priority for WR
deal_breakers: Poor hands and drop rate
One-dimensional game — must have multiple ways to win
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: True elite BPA value — Lamb falling to 17 despite already having Cooper and Gallup. They do NOT reach for WRs to fill need.

college_production.production_floor: High for early-round WRs. Lamb had 173 catches, 3,292 yards, 32 TDs at Oklahoma. They want proven producers when investing early capital.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers early breakout. Lamb was a true freshman All-American.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Will adjust for system — Flournoy from SE Missouri State had less raw volume but showed traits at Senior Bowl.

examples: 2020 R1.17 — CeeDee Lamb: 6-2, 198, Oklahoma. 173 catches, 3292 yards, 32 TDs. Pure BPA — already had Cooper and Gallup. 4x Pro Bowler.
2024 R6.216 — Ryan Flournoy: 6-2, SE Missouri State. 118 catches, 1823 yards. Athletic, physical. McClay said he 'stood out at the combine.'
2022 R3.88 — Jalen Tolbert: 6-1, South Alabama. 82 catches, 1474 yards senior year. Physical big-play WR from small school.
2023 R7.244 — Jalen Brooks: 6-2, South Carolina. Late-round developmental pick.
2025: No WR drafted despite known need. McClay: 'player acquisition is a cycle that is always ongoing.'

WR.confidence_note: Only one high draft pick (Lamb) at WR in 12 years. The Cowboys fill this position through trades (Amari Cooper, Jonathan Mingo, George Pickens) more than the draft.

TE.archetype_summary: Hybrid blocking-receiving tight end who can work inline and displaced. Dallas wants TEs who can contribute as blockers in the run game while also threatening as receivers. Jake Ferguson (R4, 2022) and Luke Schoonmaker (R2, 2023) represent the two sides of this: Ferguson is the receiving threat, Schoonmaker is the blocking-first complement. The archetype is a complete TE, not a move TE or pure blocker.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Vertical jump (receiving ability/explosiveness). Blocking technique on film matters more than combine testing.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Sub-4.75 preferred but not required for blocking-first TEs.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Complete TE play — must contribute in both blocking and receiving. Schoonmaker was described as a player who 'can do what the Cowboys want from their tight ends, working on the line as well as displaced.'
most_valued_traits: Inline blocking ability — must contribute in run game
Ability to work displaced from the formation as a receiving threat
least_important_traits: Elite receiving production — they don't need the TE to be the primary weapon
deal_breakers: Inability to block at the point of attack — pure move TEs are not the Dallas archetype
Poor blocking technique despite size
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: N/A — they have not reached for TEs. McClay-era TE picks have been at appropriate value.

college_production.production_floor: Low — Schoonmaker had 52 catches over two seasons in Michigan's run-heavy offense. Blocking contribution on film matters more than receiving stats.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Highly context-sensitive — Schoonmaker's low receiving numbers were adjusted for Michigan's run-first scheme.

examples: 2023 R2.58 — Luke Schoonmaker: 6-5, 251, Michigan. 52 catches over 2 seasons. Blocking-first TE. Inline and displaced versatility.
2022 R4.129 — Jake Ferguson: 6-5, 246, Wisconsin. More receiving-oriented. Became starter and earned Pro Bowl honors.
2022 UDFA — Peyton Hendershot: Indiana. Undrafted but earned roster spot. Versatile complementary TE.

TE.confidence_note: Limited TE draft investment. Schoonmaker is the only top-60 TE pick under McClay. Profile inferred from both picks and free agent patterns.

RB.archetype_summary: Dallas has a complicated history with RBs — they took Ezekiel Elliott at #4 overall (2016) but have since pivoted to finding running backs later in the draft or through free agency. The post-Elliott philosophy is clear: do not invest premium draft capital at RB. They want complementary backs found on Day 3 who offer different skill sets — a speed/receiving back paired with a power back. The Jaydon Blue/Phil Mafah pairing in 2025 is the template.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed (40 time) for smaller backs; power metrics for bigger backs. They draft complementary profiles.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Varies by archetype. Blue is the speed back. Mafah is the power back.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Complementary backfield pairing. McClay: '[Jaydon Blue] is an explosive playmaker... put him in the backfield behind what we've built on the line.'
most_valued_traits: Ball security — fumbles are a concern
least_important_traits: Pass protection — not a differentiator in their RB evaluation based on draft picks
deal_breakers: High draft cost — they will not invest premium picks at RB post-Elliott
Ball security issues
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: N/A — they explicitly avoid reaching for RBs. Tony Pollard was a 4th-round pick. Recent RBs have been Day 3.

college_production.production_floor: No minimum — Blue had 730 rushing yards in his best season playing behind Bijan Robinson and Jonathon Brooks at Texas. They value traits from limited roles.
college_production.production_trajectory: No strong preference. Will take backs who were complementary pieces in college.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — they adjust for backs who shared carries (Blue behind Robinson) or played in committee systems.

examples: 2025 R5.149 — Jaydon Blue: Texas. Speed/receiving back. 730 rushing yards, 42 catches in best season. Explosive playmaker.
2025 R7.239 — Phil Mafah: 6-1, 230, Clemson. Power back. 2800+ career rushing yards, 28 TDs. Physical complement to Blue.
2023 R6.212 — Deuce Vaughn: 5-6, 179, Kansas State. Small, shifty, speed back. Emotional family connection (father works in Cowboys personnel).
2019 R4.128 — Tony Pollard: Memphis. 4th-round complementary back who became a Pro Bowler.
2016 R1.4 — Ezekiel Elliott: Ohio State. 3x Pro Bowler. The exception — Dallas has not drafted a RB in rounds 1-3 since.

QB.archetype_summary: Dallas does not draft QBs with premium capital under McClay. Dak Prescott was a 4th-round pick in 2016. Every other QB drafted has been a late-round developmental/backup type. The philosophy is to invest in the franchise QB through other means (Prescott's development, veteran backups) and not allocate early draft capital to the position. When they do draft QBs, they want pocket passers with arm talent and competitive fire.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Arm strength and accuracy are evaluated through film and private workouts, not combine testing. Mobility is a plus but not required.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: N/A — pocket passing ability is the priority.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Pocket passer with enough mobility to extend plays. Prescott is the archetype — strong arm, competitive, can create outside the pocket but not a designed runner.
most_valued_traits: Ability to process quickly under pressure
least_important_traits: Designed rushing ability — not a priority
deal_breakers: Run-first QBs with questionable passing ability
Character or leadership concerns
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: N/A — Dallas does not reach for QBs. They find value at the position.

college_production.production_floor: Must be a multi-year starter. Prescott started 3 years at Mississippi State.
college_production.production_trajectory: Ascending production preferred — Prescott improved each year.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Values competitive fire and winning record over raw stats.

examples: 2016 R4.135 — Dak Prescott: 6-2, 238, Mississippi State. Competitive, dual-threat potential with passing-first game. 3x Pro Bowler.
2025 R7 (trade) — Joe Milton III: Acquired via trade from New England. Athletic, developmental backup.

QB.confidence_note: Only 1 significant QB drafted in McClay era. The philosophy is clear (don't invest premium capital at QB) but the archetype preference is thin. With Prescott locked in, QB is unlikely to be a draft priority unless there's a succession plan.

reach_tolerance.notes: McClay generally stays close to consensus in Round 1. Mazi Smith at 26 (2023) was the biggest reach — many boards had him as a R2 player. Tyler Guyton at 29 was roughly in line after the trade-back. In Round 2, they are more willing to reach for athletic profiles (Kneeland). McClay's board explicitly differs from public consensus — he says 'we set our board based off of our historical values' — so perceived reaches may be on-board picks.

scheme_fit.offensive_scheme: West Coast concepts with zone-blocking run game and tempo elements. Schottenheimer values a balanced, run-first approach that was more aggressive offensively than McCarthy's system. Pre-snap motion (53.8% for Turpin in Week 1 2025) was a key change. Run-game commitment with physicality at the OL. Values versatile offensive linemen who can pull and reach-block in zone concepts.
scheme_fit.defensive_scheme: Transitioning to 3-4 base in 2026 under Christian Parker (Vic Fangio coaching tree). Previously ran 4-3 from 2013-2025 under various DCs. Parker: 'Core principles, we'll be a 3-4 by nature, but 4-3 spacing will be appropriate, 4-2-5 in nickel.' Values multiple fronts and versatile EDGE players who can stand up or put hand in dirt. This scheme change significantly impacts draft needs — will need inside linebackers and versatile EDGE rushers.
scheme_fit.scheme_change_notes: MAJOR defensive scheme change from 4-3 to 3-4 in 2026. Christian Parker hired as DC in January 2026. First time Cowboys have run 3-4 base since Rob Ryan in 2012. This means: (1) EDGE players need stand-up capability, (2) ILB becomes a premium need, (3) Nose tackle and 3-4 DE body types may be targeted. McClay has said he's 'excited' about scouting for the new scheme and is adapting his board accordingly.

psychological_profile.risk_description: Moderate risk-taker. McClay has taken calculated risks on injured players (Jaylon Smith 2016 — torn ACL/nerve damage; Shavon Revel 2025 — torn ACL; Randy Gregory 2015 — character) and small-school players (Kneeland from Western Michigan, Flournoy from SE Missouri State). However, the Mazi Smith miss appears to have shifted them slightly toward safer picks — Tyler Booker at 12 (2025) was described as 'the safest pick' available. Jerry Jones at 2026 combine: 'we got to go this year' regarding avoiding injury risk players.
psychological_profile.recency_description: Moderate-to-high recency bias. After the Cowboys had the worst defense in the NFL in 2025 (30th in yards, last in points), the 2026 offseason is entirely defense-focused. Stephen Jones: 'we've got work to do on the defensive side of the ball.' Jerry Jones vowed to 'bust the budget' and 'do some dramatic things.' The Parsons trade and defensive collapse is driving aggressive defensive spending in 2026 through both FA and draft.
psychological_profile.sunk_cost_behavior: Willing to move on from busts. Trevon Diggs was waived during the 2025 season despite being a former 2nd-round pick and Pro Bowler. Mazi Smith has underperformed but they continue to look for alternatives. They released Ezekiel Elliott from his massive contract. Not a sunk-cost organization.
psychological_profile.combine_description: McClay values measurables as a confirm/deny tool. He explicitly said: 'You measure everything, height, weight. You see them run and do all that stuff and get the measurables, but how do they play the game of football?' Also: 'traits get you in trouble' and 'traits are nothing if you don't know what to do with them.' The Mazi Smith bust reinforced this — traits without film evidence don't work. The Cowboys use measurable thresholds as eliminators (minimum arm length for CBs, EDGE, OTs) but film is the primary evaluation tool.
psychological_profile.character_description: Moderate-to-high character standards with exceptions for value. McClay is described as 'a great judge of people and character.' Most picks under his tenure are high-character players. However, they have taken calculated risks: Randy Gregory (2nd round, character flag), Sam Williams (2nd round, off-field history), Jaylon Smith (injury, not character). The general tendency is to avoid major character risks in rounds 1-2 and take occasional fliers on Day 3.
psychological_profile.quarterback_philosophy: Dallas does not draft QBs with premium capital. They invested in Dak Prescott (4th round, 2016) and have built around him for a decade. No QB has been drafted in rounds 1-3 under McClay. The approach is to find the franchise QB at value and invest elsewhere. Prescott is now on a long-term deal through 2028.

college_production_preferences.production_vs_projection: Split between production and projection, but trending toward production-first after the Mazi Smith lesson. McClay's philosophy has always been a blend, but the 2023-2025 picks suggest a recalibration toward proven producers (Booker's 970 pass-blocking snaps, Ezeiruaku's 16.5 sacks) over raw athletic traits without production. When forced to choose, production + traits > traits alone.

production_thresholds_by_position.WR: No early-round WR drafted without major college production. Lamb had 173 career catches. Tolbert had 82 catches as a senior.
production_thresholds_by_position.RB: No minimum — will draft low-usage backs from committee systems (Blue behind Robinson at Texas).
production_thresholds_by_position.EDGE: No hard sack minimum. Kneeland had only 12.5 career sacks. Will bet on traits from small schools. But higher production (Ezeiruaku's 16.5 sacks) gets higher draft capital investment.
production_thresholds_by_position.CB: No clear statistical threshold — values film-based evaluation over INT totals. DaRon Bland had modest college stats.
production_thresholds_by_position.OL: No strict production threshold. Guyton had only 15 career starts. Tyler Smith was from Tulsa. They bet on traits at OL more than any other position.
production_thresholds_by_position.LB: Prefers high tackle volume for ILBs. Vander Esch: 141 tackles. But coverage ability on film trumps tackle numbers.
production_thresholds_by_position.DT: Low production acceptable if traits are elite (Mazi Smith). But this approach burned them — future DT picks likely need more production evidence.

college_production_preferences.notes: McClay's explicit statement — 'traits are nothing if you don't know what to do with them' — signals a post-Mazi Smith recalibration. The 2024-2025 picks trended toward players who both tested well AND produced (Ezeiruaku: elite production + solid traits; Booker: elite production + good size). Pure traits-over-production bets appear limited to Day 3 going forward.

WR.notes: Has drafted WRs from 5-6 (Vaughn, technically RB) to 6-2 (Lamb). No rigid physical requirements at WR.

measurable_thresholds.threshold_type: Hard cutoffs at premium positions (CB arm length, EDGE arm length, OT arm length). Softer preferences at non-premium positions. McClay explicitly references 'criteria or standards that have been tried and true' for positional measurables, indicating systematic thresholds exist.

size_preferences_by_position.DT: Prefers 310+ for any interior role. Mazi Smith (323), Jay Toia (325), Justin Rogers (346). Bigger is better at DT.
size_preferences_by_position.LB: Wide range — from 229 (Overshown) to 256 (Vander Esch). Athletic profile matters more than size.
size_preferences_by_position.S: Prefers 6-0+ safeties — consistent with free agent targets (Kearse 6-4, Hooker 6-1).
size_preferences_by_position.EDGE: 248-270 range. Must have length (34"+ arms). Not undersized speed rushers.

measurable_thresholds.athletic_testing_favorites: McClay appears to weight vertical jump and broad jump as explosiveness proxies across positions. 3-cone drill matters for EDGE (Kneeland's 7.02 was elite) and CB. Bench press is noted for DTs (Smith's 34 reps were a selling point). The Cowboys use the combine to confirm what they see on film — not as a standalone evaluation tool.
measurable_thresholds.measurables_vs_film: Film-first but measurables serve as eliminators. McClay: 'how do they play the game of football? That's what you're looking for. It's a combination of the two.' The Mazi Smith bust (elite measurables, poor film-to-NFL translation) reinforced that traits without film evidence are dangerous. McClay's latest comments — 'traits get you in trouble' — are the clearest signal that film has gained relative weight in their process.

round_strategy.round_1_strategy: Premium positions, high-floor starters preferred. In the McClay era, Round 1 picks have been: OL (Martin, Tyler Smith, Guyton, Booker), EDGE/DL (Charlton, Mazi Smith), LB (Vander Esch, Parsons), WR (Lamb), RB (Elliott), CB (Byron Jones). They prioritize positions where getting it right creates multi-year starters. They are willing to trade back in Round 1 to gain extra picks (2021: 10→12; 2024: 24→29). When a BPA falls unexpectedly (Lamb at 17), they take the value regardless of roster construction.
round_strategy.round_2_3_strategy: This is where they address secondary needs and take higher-upside bets. Round 2 picks include EDGE rushers (Lawrence, Kneeland, Ezeiruaku, Sam Williams), TEs (Schoonmaker), and high-risk/high-reward players (Jaylon Smith R2 with torn ACL). Round 3 is for LBs (Overshown, Liufau), CBs (Revel), and positional depth at OL (Beebe). Need-filling becomes more prominent in rounds 2-3.
round_strategy.day_3_strategy: Developmental athletes, positional depth, and complementary pieces. Day 3 is for: (1) OL depth — they take at least one OL on Day 3 almost every year, (2) Athletic fliers at skill positions — Flournoy, Tolbert, Deuce Vaughn, (3) Run-stuffing DTs as developmental projects — Toia, Akingbesote, Rogers, (4) Special teams contributors. McClay explicitly labels Day 3 picks as 'traits' plays: 'At this point of a draft, it's about traits.'
round_strategy.compensatory_pick_usage: No clearly differentiated strategy for comp picks vs. regular picks based on available data. They have traded comp picks (5th-round comp for Stephon Gilmore) and used them for standard selections.

OL.note: Signature position. Martin, Frederick, Tyler Smith, Beebe, Booker all became starters. Strongest evaluation area.

EDGE.note: Mixed — Lawrence and Parsons were home runs. Charlton was a bust. Sam Williams had issues. Kneeland and Ezeiruaku still developing.

LB.note: Parsons was generational. Vander Esch was good when healthy. Jaylon Smith had moments. Overshown, Liufau still developing.

CB.note: Diggs and DaRon Bland were major hits. Byron Jones was solid. Claiborne, Kelvin Joseph, Nahshon Wright were misses.

WR.note: CeeDee Lamb is elite. Tolbert and Flournoy have not developed into starters. WR evaluation outside of Lamb is weak.

DT.note: Mazi Smith was a clear bust. Day 3 DTs have not developed. Blind spot.

RB.note: Elliott was elite early. Pollard was a strong hit. Vaughn and recent Day 3 picks have not panned out.

TE.note: Ferguson was a hit (Pro Bowl). Schoonmaker is a rotational player.

S.note: Not a draft priority. They find safeties in free agency.

blind_spots: DT evaluation — Mazi Smith bust and all Day 3 DTs have failed to develop. The 2026 scheme change may help by changing what they need from DTs.
WR evaluation beyond BPA blue chips — when Lamb fell, they nailed it. But mid-round WR picks have not hit.
Safety evaluation — essentially outsourced to free agency, suggesting internal evaluation at the position is not trusted.
strengths: OL evaluation is elite — best in the NFL over this span with 15 Pro Bowlers since 2014.
LB/EDGE athleticism identification — Parsons, Vander Esch, Lawrence were all excellent evaluations of athletic, versatile defenders.
CB value picks — DaRon Bland in Round 5 and Caelen Carson in Round 5 suggest strong late-round CB evaluation.

free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_spending_philosophy: Historically conservative — Dallas has been known for drafting and developing rather than spending in free agency. Jerry Jones vowed at 2026 combine to 'spend more money in free agency than we have.' Stephen Jones: 'We certainly think we'll be involved in free agency.' The 2025 offseason (Javonte Williams extension, George Pickens franchise tag) showed more willingness to spend than prior years. Still, the Cowboys are primarily a build-through-the-draft organization.
free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_draft_correlation: When they address a position in free agency, they deprioritize it in the draft. In 2023, they traded for Stephon Gilmore and Brandin Cooks, then went need-based in the draft (DT, TE) instead of CB or WR. In 2025, they had Parsons, then traded him — which opened up EDGE as a draft priority (Ezeiruaku R2). McClay: 'We set our board regardless of free agency' — but the actual pick selections clearly reflect which positions remain as needs after FA.
free_agency_draft_interaction.roster_construction_philosophy: Build through the draft at premium positions (OL, EDGE, LB, CB). Supplement through free agency and trades at non-premium positions (S, DT, WR2, RB). Use Day 3 picks and UDFA signings for depth. McClay's explicit philosophy: the draft board is constructed independently, but the actual draft selections reflect a roster-aware approach that accounts for FA signings.

notable_patterns: OL has been drafted in Round 1 in 5 of last 7 drafts where Dallas had a 1st-round pick (Martin 2014, Tyler Smith 2022, Guyton 2024, Booker 2025 — plus Frederick 2013 just before McClay era).
At least one OL drafted in 12 of last 15 drafts.
EDGE/DL consistently addressed in Round 2: Lawrence (2014), Sam Williams (2022), Kneeland (2024), Ezeiruaku (2025).
72+ draft-day trades under Jerry Jones ownership. Frequent traders, primarily trading back.
Power 5 and small-school mix: McClay is willing to draft from any level of competition (Western Michigan, Fresno State, SE Missouri State, Tulsa) if traits are exceptional.
Character is valued but calculated risks are taken — Gregory (R2, substance abuse), Jaylon Smith (R2, major injury), Sam Williams (R2, off-field).
2026 draft will be defense-heavy — first time with two 1st-round picks since 2008. Stephen Jones explicitly said LB and secondary are priorities.

biggest_reaches.context: Addressed run defense need. Topped Athletic's Freaks List. Elite combine. Cowboys went need-based despite Jones family rhetoric about BPA. Clear bust.
T.J. Watt was available. Cowboys chose Charlton for 4-3 scheme fit. Charlton was a bust. This pick haunts the organization and informed the shift toward athletic versatility at EDGE.

biggest_steals.context: Became All-Pro in 2023 with record-setting 5 pick-six season. 5th-round steal. McClay's best late-round find.
Franchise quarterback found in the 4th round. 3x Pro Bowler. Perhaps the single best pick of the McClay era by value.
Fell unexpectedly. McClay took pure BPA despite having Cooper and Gallup. 4x Pro Bowler.

confidence_notes: 12 drafts of McClay-era data (2014-2025) provide a robust behavioral model. The major variable for 2026 is the defensive scheme change to 3-4 under Christian Parker, which could shift positional priorities at EDGE, ILB, and DT. Jerry Jones's increased willingness to spend in free agency could reduce the number of need positions on draft day. Two first-round picks (12 and 20) provide unusual flexibility.
